sources.episode_title: Becoming evidence-guided | Itamar Gilad (Gmail, YouTube, Microsoft)
mental_models: Opinion-based development — building based on belief and conviction rather than validated evidence
Evidence-guided company — organizations that prioritize customer focus, data, and learning over top-down conviction
Plan and execute — a top-down playbook where a big idea is committed to and executed without sufficient validation
Fail fast — the principle of quickly testing ideas and killing or pivoting projects that don't work
Fake door / smoke test / Wizard of Oz testing — simulating a product experience to gather user evidence before building
Dog-fooding — using your own product internally as an early validation mechanism
User-centric goal setting — anchoring product development in clearly defined user problems before generating solutions
key_quotes: We come up with an idea, we believe in it, all the indications show it's good. Maybe the early tests show it's good, then we just go all in and we try to implement it — and I made this very mistake many times as the product manager.
For me, this was kind of a turning point — I felt we need to adopt a different system.

Google was what I call an evidence guided company. So essentially it put a high premium on focusing on customers, coming up with a lot of ideas, looking at the data, looking at how these ideas actually worked out.
Had we kept fail fast it would've really have helped Google+, if we had this mentality.

It turned out that a lot of people were receiving social notifications and promotions, etc, and most of them were very passive. They weren't clearing their inbox, they were just living in this world of clutter.

guest_context.background: Itamar Gilad is a former Google product manager who worked on Gmail, including the tabbed inbox feature. He now coaches and writes about evidence-based product development.

sources.quote: Yeah, I guess the final thought is if someone wants to do a startup and doesn't know where to start, just to give you permission to talk to potential customers and try to pre-sell something before you write code and have those conversations…
sources.episode_title: Lessons from 1,000+ YC startups: Resilience, tar pit ideas, pivoting, more | Dalton Caldwell (YC)
mental_models: Just Don't Die — the mantra of irrational persistence as the core startup survival strategy
Coaching the fundamentals — elite performance is sustained by constant reinforcement of basics, not novel insights
Irrational continuation — successful founders persist past the rational point of quitting
Near-death experiences as prerequisites — multiple near-failures are a common pattern before breakthrough success
The Good Pivot feels like going home — a pivot is right when it moves toward founder expertise and comfort
Overnight success illusion — what looks like sudden success is actually years of invisible grinding and near-failures
Love your startup test — whether founders enjoy their product, customers, and co-founders is a key signal for whether to continue
Opportunity cost of saving face — continuing a startup purely to avoid embarrassment is a misallocation of finite life
key_quotes: One of my mantras is just don't die. Just keep your startup going. Just keep going.
A good pivot is like going home. It's warmer, it's closer to something that you're an expert at.
The underlying theme is that rationally the founder should have given up at some point.
It's not that I said some Ninja 5D chess move that they never would've thought of before. It's just the constant affirmation that continuing to keep going and doing high quality reps is the game.

Doing something that makes you miserable and the only reason you're doing it is to avoid losing face and you know in your heart is not going to work — that seems like a pretty big opportunity cost on literally your life.

guest_context.background: Dalton Caldwell is a Partner at Y Combinator, where he works closely with early-stage founders across batches. He is known for his direct, fundamentals-focused coaching philosophy and deep experience evaluating thousands of startup applications.

mental_models: Skills-first hiring — translating work experience into transferable skills to rebalance labor markets across titles and industries

In-network vs. out-of-network content strategy — feed must serve both relationship-based opportunity signals and broader knowledge/advice content

Everyone knows how that decision is going to be made. It's all about how we're connecting people to economic opportunity.

We made a pretty big push in something we call skills-first hiring. This was the idea that we could translate people's experiences into a set of skills, and by that we could help them really start balancing the marketplace with a much different system.

I don't think it's any place near declaring victory in any part of this product. I think one of the beauty of working with that vision of connecting people to opportunity, there is always some piece of friction.
As long as we stay focused on that and each of our decisions start moving in that direction, hopefully the product will continue to deliver.

guest_context.background: Hari Srinivasan is a product leader at LinkedIn focused on connecting people to economic opportunity through jobs, learning, and the feed. He has driven initiatives around skills-first hiring and the algorithmic systems that power LinkedIn's member experience.

sources.quote: Yeah. I really think it's more of... The mindset you go into to almost any problem or opportunity is the saying of, "Strong opinions weakly held." I think it's totally fine to have some of these assumptions or hypotheses going into a lot of…
sources.episode_title: “Dumbest idea I’ve heard” to $100M ARR: Inside the rise of Gamma | Grant Lee (co-founder)
mental_models: Growth-first founding mindset — baking growth into product strategy from day one in competitive categories
Micro-influencer marketing — prioritizing thousands of niche, trusted creators over large-follower celebrity endorsers
Authentic influencer voice — enabling creators to tell the product story in their own words rather than scripting ads
Same-day prototype-to-experiment loop — ideating, building, recruiting users, and running experiments within a single day
Honest PMF signal reading — resisting the temptation to fool yourself into believing you have product-market fit without strong organic word of mouth
Adversity as strategy clarifier — using harsh investor rejection as a forcing function to sharpen growth thinking
Wildfire growth through trust networks — compounding organic spread by seeding micro-communities where the product is genuinely useful
key_quotes: That has to be the worst pitch, worst idea I have ever heard. Not only are you trying to go against incumbents, you're going against incumbents that have massive distribution. You are never going to succeed.
I don't come from a growth background. So if I can learn growth, anybody can learn growth.
You want to be able to have them tell your story but in their voice.

You're much better doing the hard thing, which is hard to scale, finding the thousands of micro influencers that have an audience where your product maybe is actually useful. People really trust what they say. That ends up becoming this wildfire that can spread really, really fast.

We would have an idea in the morning, come up with some sort of functional prototype, recruit a bunch of people that are legitimately good prospective users, but have zero skin in the game, ship fast so people can start playing with it.

guest_context.background: Grant Lee is a first-time founder who co-founded Gamma in 2020, a presentation and content creation tool competing with established incumbents. He led the company through early fundraising challenges and developed a growth-first mindset despite having no prior growth background.

sources.episode_title: How Revolut trains world-class PMs: The “Local CEO” model, raw intellect & building wow products
mental_models: Local CEO / Product Owner as end-to-end business owner
Lean headcount with aggressive growth appetite
Raw intellect and hunger over experience in hiring
99% done is closer to 0% than 100% (completion threshold model)
Three types of product owners: UX, Technical, and Data Science
Cross-functional team structure with dual reporting (line manager + functional manager)
Never compromise on quality and UX even when cutting scope
Deep root-cause analysis as a core product competency
key_quotes: If something is 99% done, it's closer to 0% rather than 100%.
Revolut values way more raw intellect and this unquenched hunger to build things rather than experience.
We can cut down the product in terms of functionality to just most critical features, but we will never compromise on the quality and UX and the aesthetics.
Product owners are central to the company and its growth. They are end-to-end responsible for the product, for this part of the business, and for their customers to be happy.
There are no obvious things and you need to get to the root cause of the problem really, really deep to understand what will work and what will not work.

guest_context.background: Dmitry Zlokazov is a product leader at Revolut, the London-founded fintech company that challenges traditional banks across 50 countries with multi-currency and financial services products. He oversees product ownership culture and strategy at a company of 6,000–7,000 employees operating across diverse global markets.

sources.episode_title: Hard-won lessons building 0 to 1 inside Atlassian | Tanguy Crusson (Head of Jira Product Discovery)
mental_models: Artificial scarcity to replicate startup urgency inside large companies
Separating incubation-stage metrics from established-product metrics
High-speed train momentum — show enough velocity that stakeholders don't interfere
Network-based flat decision-making (empowerment over hierarchy)
Staged incubator model for internal new bets
Distribution leverage — building on top of existing customer base rather than starting from zero
50/50 success rate as a realistic benchmark for innovation portfolios
Customer story plus data pairing to communicate progress and buy stakeholder trust
key_quotes: The company has a tendency to over-invest. Startups have the benefit of starving, and so you need to create scarcity.
Be very clear about what we're testing, doing that with data, doing that with personal customer stories, give people a sense of velocity and speed. No one wants to fuck with a high-speed train.
My track record at Atlassian has been 50/50 at best. Jira Project Discovery is actually my first, what I would call big success here.
A $100 million business is a good start basically. In most companies out there, $100 million business is a home run. For us, it's not like that.
I needed the rest of the company to go away so we could get the autonomy to test the things that we needed, but it's not going to scale. That is not going to respect all design guidelines.

guest_context.background: Tanguy Crusson has spent roughly 10 years at Atlassian working on bootstrapping new products, including HipChat, Stride, and most recently Jira Product Discovery, which he launched through Atlassian's internal incubator. His work focuses on building new bets inside a large enterprise, navigating the unique challenges of corporate innovation.

sources.episode_title: Linear’s secret to building beloved B2B products | Nan Yu (Head of Product)
mental_models: Speed as competence, not sloppiness — fast output signals mastery, not rushing
10% time budget rule — a workable prototype testing a key hypothesis should exist after just 10% of the allotted time
Iteration volume as product quality driver — more iterations directly correlates to better end product
First version as directional hypothesis, not final output — no expectation of perfection in v1
Controlled risk-taking — accepting imperfection early makes development cheaper and faster
Feature bloat as IC morale killer — saying no to certain feature requests protects developer experience
Empathy through shared pain — 'feeling bad the same way customers feel bad' as a product north star
key_quotes: If you look at people who are at the pinnacle of their craft, you can basically tell how good the output is going to be of their work product by how fast they're going.

After week one you have something that works that tests some kind of key hypothesis internally so that you can feel like is this thing actually panning out the way we expect it to or did we have some crazy incorrect assumption?
My goal is to feel bad in the same way that customers feel bad.
The stuff that we absolutely have to say no to is the exact kind of thing that leads to this bloatedness that makes ICs hate their lives.
There's no expectation that the first version of it is going to be great. That is not in the cards.

guest_context.background: Nan Yu is a product leader at Linear, a project management tool built for software teams. She focuses on product quality, speed of iteration, and building tools that restore joy to the day-to-day workflows of engineers and designers.

sources.episode_title: A founder’s guide to crisis management | Uri Levine (Waze co-founder, serial entrepreneur)
mental_models: Fall in love with the problem, not the solution
Problem as North Star — using a clearly defined problem to guide all startup decisions and reduce course deviation
Problem-first storytelling — leading with the problem or value created rather than the solution or product
User validation at scale — speaking with ~100 people (especially strangers) to confirm a problem is real
Value creation as the core purpose of entrepreneurship — the simplest path is solving a real problem
The 30-day hiring check — re-evaluating new hires after 30 days with 'would I hire this person today?'
Presentation structure priority — the first and last slides are the most important; lead with your strongest point
key_quotes: Fall in love with the problem, and then actually what you're trying to do is engage everyone else to fall in love with the same problem.

When you fall in love with the problem, the problem is going to serve as the North Star of your journey, and when you have a North Star, you're going to make less deviation from the course and you are way more likely to become successful.

If I will tell you I'm going to help you to avoid traffic jams, then you do care, and when your customer cares, they want you to be successful, and when they want you to be successful, they are going to help you to become successful.

Every time that you hire someone new, mark your calendars for 30 days down the road and ask yourself one question, knowing what I know today, would I hire this person? If the answer is no, fire them immediately.

If you start with the story with, 'The problem we are solving is,' then you focus on the problem. If your story starts with, 'The value that we create for you is,' then you focus on the user. The last two are way better than focus on the solution.

guest_context.background: Uri Levine is a serial entrepreneur and co-founder of Waze, the crowd-sourced navigation app acquired by Google. He is also the author of 'Fall in Love with the Problem, Not the Solution,' a book on entrepreneurship and startup building.

How do you validate an idea before build.rewritten_query: How do you validate an idea before building it?

voices.summary: Itamar argues that organizations massively overestimate how much they need to build in order to learn. Instead of launching elaborate pseudo-MVPs, teams should treat learning as a distinct, low-cost step that happens before full product development. The key shift is recognizing that you can validate core assumptions without committing to months of engineering work.

Dalton's advice is simple and tactical: start with customer validation before writing any code. Specifically, try to pre-sell your idea to potential customers and have real conversations with them. This approach gives founders and PMs genuine signal on demand and willingness to pay before any significant investment is made.

Hari highlights that one of the hardest parts of validation isn't doing it — it's knowing what 'validated' actually looks like in your organization. There's no universal bar, and most teams struggle to define the threshold at which an idea has enough evidence to move forward. Getting clarity on that bar is itself a critical part of the validation process.

Grant frames idea validation as a mindset rather than a process: go in with strong hypotheses but hold them loosely, and always look for the fastest, cheapest way to test them. As a founder or PM, your job is to systematically build conviction through evidence. The goal is to move from assumption to validated belief without over-investing before the signal is clear.

Dmitry emphasizes that poor validation creates a dangerous ambiguity: when a product fails to get traction, you can't tell if the idea itself is wrong or if the product quality masked a good idea. His solution is to always build with high quality and strong UX, even in early versions, so that a lack of traction is a clean signal that the underlying idea needs rethinking.

Tanguy's experience building inside Atlassian shows the importance of validating whether users will engage with a new workflow or category before building the full system. With Jira for Discovery, the team tested whether product managers would actually use a pre-commitment space for prioritization debates before committing to deep infrastructure. Early-stage validation for 0-to-1 products must focus on behavior change, not just stated interest.

Nan offers a build-to-learn perspective: sometimes the fastest way to validate is to build the most extreme, purest version of your idea immediately, because you know early versions won't be the final answer anyway. Shipping that extreme version quickly surfaces real feedback that no amount of conversation can replicate. It's a high-speed iteration approach rather than a pre-build discovery approach.

Uri prescribes a specific, actionable validation method: talk to approximately 100 potential users, and critically, most of them must be strangers — not friends or family. Comfortable conversations with people who want to support you produce false signal. Real validation comes from people with no emotional stake in your success who still find the problem worth solving.
